In His final hours with the disciples, Jesus performs an act so scandalous that Peter recoils from it. The Teacher, the Lord, the one who commissioned them to follow—He strips off His garments, wraps a towel around His waist like a slave, and washes their feet. This is not incidental detail. In that culture, foot-washing was the work of servants, the lowest task in any household. Yet Jesus does it with full knowledge of who He is: the eternal Son through whom all things were made, the one to whom the Father has delivered all authority. He does not wash their feet despite His power and position; He washes their feet because of it. This is the shape of His kingdom made visible. Peter's resistance reveals the scandal at the heart of the gospel—that our Lord refuses to distance Himself from the work of service, that He does not pull away from humble labor even when He stands on the threshold of His greatest glory.

But Jesus is not performing a symbolic gesture divorced from reality. When He tells Peter that unless he is washed, he has no part with Him, He is speaking about something far deeper than clean feet. The washing He offers is cleansing from sin itself—the very work He is about to accomplish through His death. Yet even as He makes this astonishing offer of love, Judas sits among them, already claimed by betrayal. Jesus knows. He sees the one Satan has entered, and He allows him to remain at the table, to receive bread from His own hand. This is the terrible weight of the moment: Jesus loves His own to the very end, even knowing that one of them will hand Him over to death. His love is not contingent on their worthiness or their faithfulness. It precedes their failure.

What follows is a command that cuts to the heart of discipleship: love one another as He has loved them. This love is not sentimental affection but costly, sacrificial, offered without guarantee of return or gratitude. By this, Jesus says, the world will know they are His disciples—not by their eloquence, their certainty, or their strength, but by their willingness to love as they have been loved. And then Peter, still reeling from the foot-washing, boasts that he will lay down his life for Jesus. Before the night is through, he will deny even knowing Him. Jesus' prediction is not cruel; it is honest. It strips away Peter's confidence in his own devotion and forces him to confront a truth he must learn: he cannot follow Christ through sheer force of will. He will need grace. He will need to be washed clean again.

As you encounter this chapter, ask yourself what form of service you are still too proud to embrace, what kind of love you are still unwilling to offer to those who may betray you. Jesus does not ask His disciples to do what He has not first done. He sets the example in absolute humility, and then He demands nothing less from those who claim His name. The word He speaks—wash one another's feet—remains His word to you. The question is whether you will obey, knowing that the very love He commands will cost you something, and may be repaid with ingratitude.
